title: Words are all you need? Language as an approximation for human similarity judgments

structured.summary: This paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of over 600 pre-trained models across images, audio, and video to approximate human similarity judgments. It introduces a novel, scalable language-based method using captions and adaptive tag collection (STEP-Tag) combined with large language model embeddings, demonstrating superior performance over deep neural network embeddings alone and enabling efficient similarity approximation at scale.

strengths.point: Comprehensive large-scale benchmark across multiple modalities
strengths.evidence: Evaluated 611 pre-trained models on images, audio, and video to assess their ability to approximate human similarity judgments.

strengths.point: Novel, modality-agnostic, and scalable language-based similarity approximation
strengths.evidence: Proposed O(N) methods using captions and adaptive tags embedded by LLMs, outperforming DNN-only methods and reducing annotation cost from O(N²) to O(N).

strengths.point: Development and validation of the STEP-Tag adaptive tagging pipeline
strengths.evidence: Iterative crowdsourcing method improved tag quality and diversity, leading to better similarity prediction than standard dataset labels.

strengths.point: Stacked embeddings combining DNN and LLM representations yield best performance
strengths.evidence: Stacked methods consistently outperformed language-only and DNN-only baselines across all tested modalities.

strengths.point: Release of a large behavioral dataset and practical guide
strengths.evidence: Provided 206,339 human judgments and similarity matrices for large audio and video datasets, facilitating future research.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison to prior language-based semantic similarity methods
weaknesses.evidence: Lacks evaluation against classical distributional semantics or word embedding approaches like Word2Vec or GloVe applied to tags or captions.
Missing specifics on LLM models used, embedding extraction procedures, crowdsourcing setup, and tuning of scaling parameters in stacked methods.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics restricted to correlation with human similarity judgments
weaknesses.evidence: No downstream task evaluations such as retrieval accuracy or clustering quality to demonstrate practical utility.

weaknesses.point: No statistical significance testing reported for method comparisons
weaknesses.evidence: Results lack confidence intervals or hypothesis tests, making robustness of observed differences unclear.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of STEP-Tag design choices and scalability
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation on tag quantity versus quality effects; scalability to very large stimulus sets not discussed.

weaknesses.point: Potential biases due to English-only, US-based participants and language data
weaknesses.evidence: Acknowledged but not deeply analyzed; limits generalizability to other languages and cultures.

weaknesses.point: Ambiguity in description of Word-Frequency Analysis (WFA) method
weaknesses.evidence: Unclear how word frequencies are computed, normalized, or converted into similarity scores.

weaknesses.point: Preliminary and underexplored GPT-3 prompting results
weaknesses.evidence: Mentioned promising outcomes but lacks detailed experiments and comparisons.

questions.question: How does the proposed language-based similarity approximation compare quantitatively to classical semantic similarity methods using word embeddings?
questions.motivation: Establishing this comparison is critical to validate the novelty and effectiveness of the language-based approach.

questions.question: What are the effects of tag quantity versus tag quality on similarity approximation performance in STEP-Tag?
questions.motivation: Understanding this trade-off can guide efficient tag collection strategies and improve method design.

questions.question: How well do the approximated similarity matrices perform on downstream tasks such as retrieval or clustering?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating practical utility beyond correlation with human judgments would strengthen the impact of the approach.

questions.question: How scalable is the STEP-Tag pipeline for datasets with tens or hundreds of thousands of stimuli?
questions.motivation: Assessing scalability is important for real-world applicability to large-scale datasets.

questions.question: How does the approach generalize to non-English languages and culturally diverse populations?
questions.motivation: Ensuring broader applicability and fairness requires evaluation beyond English and US-centric data.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison to prior language-based semantic similarity methods. Lacks evaluation against classical distributional semantics or word embedding approaches like Word2Vec or GloVe applied to tags or captions
critique_points.point_002: Limited methodological details affecting reproducibility. Missing specifics on LLM models used, embedding extraction procedures, crowdsourcing setup, and tuning of scaling parameters in stacked methods
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation metrics restricted to correlation with human similarity judgments. No downstream task evaluations such as retrieval accuracy or clustering quality to demonstrate practical utility
critique_points.point_004: No statistical significance testing reported for method comparisons. Results lack confidence intervals or hypothesis tests, making robustness of observed differences unclear
critique_points.point_005: Limited exploration of STEP-Tag design choices and scalability. No ablation on tag quantity versus quality effects; scalability to very large stimulus sets not discussed
critique_points.point_006: Potential biases due to English-only, US-based participants and language data. Acknowledged but not deeply analyzed; limits generalizability to other languages and cultures
critique_points.point_007: Ambiguity in description of Word-Frequency Analysis (WFA) method. Unclear how word frequencies are computed, normalized, or converted into similarity scores
critique_points.point_008: Preliminary and underexplored GPT-3 prompting results. Mentioned promising outcomes but lacks detailed experiments and comparisons
